title: Unit 03 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Privacy, Security & Cyber Safety.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of online privacy settings?

choices.text: To make websites load faster
choices.feedback: Privacy settings control who can see or access information, not website speed.

choices.text: To organize files on a device
choices.feedback: File organization is different from online privacy control.

choices.text: To help protect personal information from unwanted access
choices.feedback: Correct. Privacy settings help limit access to personal information and content.

choices.text: To improve battery life on phones
choices.feedback: Battery life is unrelated to the main purpose of privacy settings.

questions.prompt: Which password is the strongest example?

choices.text: password123
choices.feedback: This password is predictable and easy to guess.

choices.text: Emma2026
choices.feedback: Using names and common numbers makes passwords weaker.

choices.text: qwerty
choices.feedback: This is a very common and weak password.

choices.text: R7!mQ2#zLp9
choices.feedback: Correct. A strong password is longer and uses a mix of letters, numbers, and symbols.

questions.prompt: Using the same password for multiple accounts increases security because it is easier to remember.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Using the same password across accounts creates greater risk if one account is compromised.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. Reusing passwords makes multiple accounts vulnerable if one password is exposed.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest warning sign that an email may be a phishing attempt?

choices.text: It includes a school announcement you expected
choices.feedback: Expected announcements are not automatically suspicious.

choices.text: It asks you to click a link immediately to avoid losing access to your account
choices.feedback: Correct. Urgent pressure and suspicious links are common phishing signs.

choices.text: It uses complete sentences and a logo
choices.feedback: Scam messages can still look polished and professional.

choices.text: It comes during the school week
choices.feedback: The timing of an email does not determine whether it is a phishing attempt.

questions.prompt: What is the safest action before downloading a file from an unfamiliar website?

choices.text: Download it quickly before the page changes
choices.feedback: Speed is not the priority when evaluating safety.

choices.text: Check whether the site and file appear trustworthy before downloading
choices.feedback: Correct. Safe downloading starts with checking credibility and possible risks.

choices.text: Ask a friend whether they think it looks fine
choices.feedback: A friend's opinion is not a substitute for evaluating safety.

choices.text: Disable security tools so the download finishes faster
choices.feedback: Disabling security tools increases risk and is not safe.

questions.prompt: A student receives repeated insulting messages in a group chat. What is the most responsible response?

choices.text: Respond with harsher insults to stop the behavior
choices.feedback: Escalating the conflict usually makes the situation worse.

choices.text: Share screenshots publicly to embarrass the sender
choices.feedback: Public retaliation can increase harm rather than solve the problem.

choices.text: Save evidence, block or report the behavior, and seek help if needed
choices.feedback: Correct. This is a responsible and safer response to cyberbullying.

choices.text: Ignore every harmful message no matter how serious it becomes
choices.feedback: Some situations require reporting and support, not total inaction.

questions.prompt: Two-factor or multi-factor authentication can improve account security.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Multi-factor authentication adds an extra layer of protection beyond a password.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Authentication methods beyond a password increase security, not reduce it.

questions.prompt: Which question is most important to ask before clicking a link or opening a message attachment?

choices.text: Will it make me more popular online?
choices.feedback: Popularity should not guide cybersecurity decisions.

choices.text: Was it sent at a convenient time of day?
choices.feedback: Timing alone does not determine whether something is safe.

choices.text: Does it come from a trusted source and seem safe to open?
choices.feedback: Correct. Verifying trust and safety is the most important first step.

choices.text: Will my friends think it is interesting?
choices.feedback: Other people's reactions are not the priority when evaluating cyber safety.
